entry.title: Re: RiskManagementService.A_BusinessPartner is annotated...
entry.content:
Hi.

You likely have something like:

srv/service.cds

using { apihub_sandbox } from './external/apihub_sandbox.cds';

service RiskManagementService
{
    entity A_BusinessPartner as
        projection on apihub_sandbox.A_BusinessPartner;
}

srv/external/apihub_sandbox.cds:

service apihub_sandbox {
    @cds.persistence.skip : true
    entity A_BusinessPartner {
        ...
    };
};
 
The entity has @CDS.persistence.skip
This tells CAP “Do NOT create a table/view for this entity”
Usually happens when you imported an external service (e.g., S/4HANA, API_BUSINESS_PARTNER)
 
So, you need to implement a a custom handler.
 
Create this file (Node.js handler):
srv/service.js
 
const cds = require('@sap/cds');
module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function () {
  const externalBP = await cds.connect.to('apihub_sandbox');
  this.on('READ', 'A_BusinessPartner', async (req) => {
    try {
      return await externalBP.run(req.query);
    } catch (err) {
      console.error('Error calling external service:', err);
      req.error(500, 'Failed to fetch Business Partners');
    }
  });
});
 
Then run 🙂

entry.title: How to make PM form sections or fields show dynamically based on EC data (without CPI or SFTP jobs)
entry.content:
Introduction

In this 45 minutes recording we demonstrate how to use SuccessFactors reporting and platform features available from Admin Center (no Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) or Integration Center jobs with SFTP) to import values from the EC profile of an employee into custom fields of a Performance template so that they can be used to drive section or fields visibility using "Performance Business Rules" (2H 2025). 

The path is cross domain Adhoc report --> MDF --> Integration Center --> PM Form custom elements --> Performance Business Rules.

 

Why this topic ? 

  • Performance Business Rules from 2H 2025 don't support the use of employee data shown in User Information standard section as starting [IF] condition. However custom fields values are supported.
  • Custom fields values in Performance Forms cannot be imported through CSV.

 

Recording link and step

  1. Add new "Launch" step of type "user" in PM form route map (user must be the same than the one who will run the Integration center jobs in step 7)
  2. Add custom fields in PM form and change their ekey to be aligned with the name you gave to the field
  3. Launch PM forms
  4. Create MDF object as per recording instructions
  5. Create Adhoc report > Table > Cross Domain > Performance Management (main domain) + Add Domain "Person and Employment info (as of date)" and query the fields shown in the recording
  6. Insert adhoc report information into MDF import template and create one import file for each custom field (please note as per recording that "/" and maybe other signs will make the integration fail so it's best to remove them)
  7. Create Integration center job(s) as per recording

 

 

Thank you to Claude for the explanations on the logic for valueKey design (critical step on which I was stuck for a long time) and also to these other blogs with slightly different business cases  : 

  • Performance form custom element update from User entity via OData API upsert in CPI
  • Performance Rating and Comments Upload via Integration Centre in SuccessFactors

 

Additional clarifications on the valueKey: if your custom element is nested under a competency or objective (which is different from my blog example where it is within its own custom section) then the valueKey to import into the MDF must have a different format. Instead of wf_sect_2_e_paygrade0 it needs to be wf_sect_2__c[N]_paygrade0 where [N] is the competency of objective ID in which the custom element is nested. So if the competency ID is 12 then you would have: wf_sect_2__c12_paygrade0. Note for this format there is a double _ after the sectionID.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

All the best,

Xavier

 

 

(If you found this blog useful please consider giving it a Kudo)

entry.title: Re: Strategies for Downtime-Optimized DMO : doDMO, doC, DMOVE2S4 for large databases
entry.content:
@rajasekhar79 

Yes it's possible. DMOVE2S4 is not limited to Rise only.
Suggestions:
- Ensure you meet the DMOVE2S4 infrastructure criteria, particularly if target and source landscape are in different regions of AWS.
- For AAS, you can scale up the EC2 to larger than its usual size and adjust parameters to leverage more resources during migration.(executing from target makes more sense if target gives you better resources).
- Try to leverage downtime-optimized feature : for migration part of conversion (doDMO), or for migration and conversion(doC) i.e. whichever you are ready for. Note that doC still needs you to do atleast one standard-conversion on copy of Prod for Customizing TR needed in doC.

entry.title: Re: Combined Picking in SAP EWM for Multiple Deliveries with Common Products
entry.content:
Dear Hendrik,

Thank you very much for your comment. I really appreciate it, especially coming from someone whose SAP EWM contributions I personally follow and respect.

You are right that the business flow shown can look very close to SAP EWM 2-step picking if we only look at the high-level process of picking common products and distributing them further.

The specific angle I intended to document here is slightly narrower: the RF “combined picking” behavior where multiple warehouse tasks are consolidated into a single quantity view on the RF screen, typically driven through queue / WO / warehouse task grouping logic.

This was actually the expectation in the scenario I was working on — not only to create separate WTs, but to have RF combine the relevant quantities so the picker sees a consolidated picking view.

I agree the wording in the blog can be improved to avoid confusion between:
1. SAP EWM 2-step picking,
2. common-product / collective picking from a business perspective, and
3. RF combined picking behavior.

I will add a clarification section with screenshots showing the RF behavior more explicitly.

Thanks again for pointing this out. This is exactly the kind of terminology precision that makes SAP EWM discussions better.

entry.title: Re: Analyze Closing Run Results for Balance Sheet Reclass...
entry.content:
Good day @FabianSCC 

Thank you for your question.

Yes, your understanding of Analyse Closing Run Results is correct. That app specifically provides analysis of the results of Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation jobs. So, it basically provides detailed analysis, reporting and reconciliation of Advance Foreign Currency Valuation jobs.

Consult these See the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/372bd396f8684a0aa642dd40ea5d67bd.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/9e63f9e340274e8f8b39e70b5684529a.html?locale=en-US

For advanced Balance Sheet reclassification, currently I am not aware of any equivalent standard analytical app with the same behaviour as Analyse Closing Run Results.

If you are considering B/S reclassification, then you can execute the standard process through Schedule General Ledger Jobs app.  

Make sure you use the following templates:

  • Post B/S Reclassification-Maturity
  • Post B/S Reclassification-Master Data Change

Note that you perform B/S reclassification with the Post Balance Sheet Reclassification job in Schedule General Ledger Jobs and that creates adjustment postings, so assets and liabilities are displayed correctly in your financial statements.

So, in simple terms:

  • Analyse Closing Run Results is for Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation.
  • There is no separate standard app with the same analysis function specifically for B/S reclassification results.
  • For B/S reclassification, the immediate result can be reviewed from the job result list or spool/output after you have run the job.
  • You can analyse the posted reclassification document by using journal entry and G/L reporting apps.
  • Should you want more specific analysis, consider creating a custom analytical reporting for Advanced Valuation results. The CDS view I_GLACCOUNTLINEITEM and Custom Analytical Queries is very helpful for that case.

 

A second option is (most practical option in my opinion) for you to analyse the posted journal entries after you your B/S reclassification job has posted accounting documents. You can use the Balance Sheet/Income Statement app and check what you need by filtering accordingly e.g., company code, ledger, fiscal year, etc.

Option 3 as mentioned previously is for you to build a custom analytical query if the standard job result list and journal entry apps do not provide enough drilldown for your case.

 

However, you should note one important limitation. The same guided states that predecessor document analysis applies to foreign currency valuation, credit risk-based impairment, and discounting, but does not apply to the reclassification program. That means a custom query can help you report the posted reclassification entries, but it may not give the same source document drilldown behaviour that you may know from Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation.

Here are references for more guidance

3202928 - How to Post Balance Sheet Reclassification - SAP for Me

3473536 - How-To Guide Using Custom Analytic Report to analyze Advanced Valuation Result - SAP for Me

3139304 - Add New Country/Region and Automatic Activation of Advanced Valuations - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition: Automating WHT for...
entry.content:
Good day @dn_eques 

Thank you for your question.

The standard automation is possible only through the supported withholding tax framework. Below are answers to your specific questions.

Question1: Is there any standard SAP approach in S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition to automate WHT determination for intercompany AP postings triggered by IDoc?

Answer: Yes, but only through standard AP withholding tax determination. The receiving company code must have extended WHT active, supplier WHT data must be maintained, and the inbound AP invoice must carry or determine the relevant WHT type/code. If the IDoc process does not determine the WHT values check KBA 3608615 for more details on the validation of the inbound AP invoice data and WHT configuration.

 

Question 2: Is there any supported design for handling the AR side when the country version does not support assigning WHT type to a condition type?

Answer: Considering you also do not want a work around, if the country version does not support WHT type to a condition type, there is no supported AR configuration to force that design. In this case, handle the WHT determination on the AP side or redesign the process using supported country specific configuration.

 

 

Question 3: If standard configuration is not sufficient, is there any recommended extensibility or workaround in Public Edition to determine whether WHT should apply at invoice or payment time?

Answer: Use standard design first: separate the WHT relevant and non WHT relevant transaction flows, maintain correct supplier WHT data, and decide whether the WHT type should apply at invoice posting or payment posting. If transaction specific automated determination is still required, check released BAdIs in Custom Logic for the exact inbound supplier invoice or accounting posting process.  

If you don’t find, then it is not supported as a cloud extension. Consider raising it as an improvement.

 

Here is what i suggest:

  1. Activate and validate extended withholding tax for the receiving company code.
  2. Check WHT type and code setup for invoice posting or payment posting.
  3. Maintain WHT data in the intercompany supplier business partner.
  4. Post the intercompany invoice through the IDoc process.
  5. Verify the WHT values in the supplier invoice and accounting document.
  6. Test a WHT relevant and non WHT relevant transaction separately.
  7. If your required transaction specific automation cannot be modelled by standard setup, consider checking released BAdIs.
  8. Finally, you may consider raising an incident if the IDoc creates the supplier invoice, but WHT values are not determined despite you having correct standard configuration.

Here are references for more guidance

3164628 - Withholding taxes being considered when clearing an invoice and its reversal document - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

3662292 - Edit withholding tax amount on outgoing or incoming Payment - SAP for Me

3228045 - Extended withholding tax with line item display - Cloud - SAP for Me

 

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Custom report : Stock In‑Out & Balance Report with Tr...
entry.content:
Good day @Grace_Li1 

Thank you for your question.

There is no standard inventory valuation logic that calculates a separate moving average price per transaction currency B or C. The official inventory valuation is based on the company code, ledger, Material Ledger, and configured valuation currencies. SAP’s moving average price logic is recalculated through valuation relevant events such as goods movement, invoice receipts, or order settlements, not as a separate MAP by each PO document currency.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/7f47a6d9441c46ac86c96fd27f6015f0/bbd9b6ce0be14516ad7868da0982024a.html?locale=en-US

For opening inventory quantity, use Material Inventory Values-Balance Summary with the key date set to the day before the reporting period start date. This app enables you to analyse inventory quantities and values by key date.

A Custom CDS View and Custom Analytical Query can be used to build a movement listing from I_MaterialDocumentItem_2 and enrich it with PO document currency and net price from I_PurchaseOrderItemAPI01. However, the step by step rolling average calculation should be handled outside the Custom Analytical Query layer as a custom management simulation, not as SAP official MAP.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/7f47a6d9441c46ac86c96fd27f6015f0/ed83861d4d8c4813b95a726a5303c401.html?locale=en-US

3123661 - Custom Field is not added to the CDS View I_MaterialDocumentItem - SAP for Me

I recommend you consider the following steps:

  1. Get opening quantity from Material Inventory values - Balance Summary.                           
  2. Use I_MaterialDocumentItem_2 to build a custom movement listing.       
  3. Join PO item currency and net price for PO based goods receipts.
  4. Sort the movement data by posting date, material document year, material document, and item.
  5. Perform the rolling simulated transaction currency average outside the analytical query.
  6. Reconcile the quantity first with the standard inventory reports.
  7. Label the output clearly as a custom management report, not as official inventory valuation.
  8. If your client needs official inventory values in another currency, review the Material Ledger and valuation currency setup instead of rebuilding MAP from PO transaction currency. See Blog: Actual Costing in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition for more details.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: COR1 - BADI OR USER EXIT FOR OPERATION CHANGES RESTRICTION
entry.content:
Hello Everyone,

I have a requirement to prevent users from modifying operation values that are copied from routing during order creation (COR1).

I am using user exit PPCO0019 (Checks for Changes to Order Operations). The exit works as expected in COR2, because it is triggered after operation changes are made. However, in COR1, the exit is called before the user edits the operation values, which prevents me from validating the changes.

Is there any alternative user exit, BAdI, or enhancement that can be used in COR1 to validate or restrict operation changes during order creation.

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Claude Code Is a Spaceship. Pi Is a Skateboard. Here's Why I Use Both
entry.content:
Pi: The Minimal Coding Agent That Puts You in Control

Most AI coding agents compete by adding more features. Web search. MCP servers. Sub-agents. Planning modes. Background tasks. IDE integrations.

Each feature makes sense on its own. Together, they can create powerful workflows, but also more complexity, more hidden behavior, and more things to learn.

Pi takes a different approach. Instead of asking, "What else should we add?", Pi asks:

"What is the smallest useful coding agent we can build?"

The result is a lightweight, open-source coding agent that starts with only a handful of tools and lets you build the rest yourself.

What Is Pi?

Pi is an open-source terminal-based coding agent built by Earendil Inc. At its core, Pi ships with just four tools:

  • Read files
  • Write files
  • Edit files
  • Run shell commands

That's it. There are no built-in sub-agents, no MCP integration, no planning mode, and no long list of default tools.

The philosophy is simple:

Adapt Pi to your workflow, not the other way around.

Unlike many coding assistants that ship as complete products, Pi is designed more like a programmable runtime for AI agents.

How to Get Started with Pi

Getting started depends on where you work.

SAP Internal
If you are inside SAP, reach out to me directly or follow the Hyperspace documentation to install and use Pi.

External
If you are outside SAP, follow the official Pi quickstart guide: https://pi.dev/docs/latest#quick-start

Why Pi Exists

Pi's creator, Mario Zechner, has been vocal about a growing trend in AI tooling: more features often mean more complexity. In his own words, he described Claude Code as:

"A spaceship with 80% of functionality I have no use for."

Whether you agree or not, the idea behind Pi is clear:

  • Keep the core small
  • Make behavior observable
  • Push customization to extensions
  • Avoid hidden state whenever possible

Pi isn't trying to be the most feature-rich coding agent. It's trying to be the most understandable one.

Extensions Are the Real Product

At first glance, Pi can look unfinished. After all, four tools isn't much. The important thing to understand is that Pi intentionally moves functionality out of the core and into extensions.

Extensions are written in TypeScript and can:

  • Add custom tools
  • Register commands
  • Listen to agent events
  • Modify prompts
  • Customize summarization
  • Extend the terminal UI
  • Change agent behavior at runtime

This is where Pi becomes interesting. Instead of configuring an agent through YAML files or shell hooks, you're extending the agent using code. The result feels less like customizing a product and more like programming a platform.

Pi's Core Philosophy: State Belongs to Files

One of the biggest differences between Pi and many other coding agents is how it handles state. Many agents maintain internal plans, todo lists, and background processes that exist only within the current session. Pi encourages a different approach.

Plans become Markdown files. Todos become Markdown files. Long-running processes run in tools like tmux. Project instructions live in files such as AGENTS.md.

This makes the agent's working memory visible, version-controlled, and shareable with the rest of your team. Instead of asking the model to remember everything, Pi encourages you to write things down.

A Typical Pi Workflow

Using Pi is intentionally simple.

pi

Then:

Build a FastAPI REST API for user management.

Pi can:

  1. Read your existing codebase
  2. Create or modify files
  3. Run build and test commands
  4. Iterate on the implementation

If you need additional capabilities, you add them through extensions rather than waiting for them to become built-in features.

Pi vs Claude Code

The easiest way to understand Pi is to compare it with Claude Code. Both are excellent tools, but they optimize for different things.

Choose Pi When...
You want full control
Pi exposes most of its behavior and keeps the core architecture intentionally small.

You enjoy customizing your tools
Pi extensions allow you to shape the agent to match your workflow rather than adapting your workflow to the tool.

You prefer transparency
There are no hidden sub-agents or complex orchestration layers running behind the scenes.

You want a hackable runtime
Pi is MIT licensed, open source, and designed to be extended.

You work primarily in the terminal
Pi is built around a terminal-first experience and feels at home in developer workflows centered around shells, tmux, and Git.

Choose Claude Code When...
You want a polished experience out of the box
Claude Code ships with a rich set of built-in capabilities and workflows.

You need enterprise features
Security controls, audit logs, access management, and organizational tooling are major strengths of Claude Code.

You want native multi-agent workflows
Claude Code includes built-in orchestration and delegation capabilities.

You prefer configuration over development
Many workflows are available immediately without needing to build custom extensions.

You use IDE-centric workflows
Claude Code integrates with a broader ecosystem of developer tools and environments.

The Real Tradeoff

The difference between Pi and Claude Code is not about which tool is better. It's about which kind of friction you prefer. Claude Code provides orchestration and convenience out of the box. Pi provides control and extensibility out of the box. One hides complexity so you can move faster. The other exposes complexity so you can shape it. Neither approach is universally right.

Final Thoughts

Pi won't appeal to everyone. That's actually one of its strengths. Instead of trying to satisfy every developer and every workflow, Pi makes a clear bet: A coding agent should be small, understandable, and programmable.

If you're looking for a feature-packed AI assistant, Claude Code may be the better fit. But if you've ever wished your coding agent behaved more like a framework than a product, Pi is absolutely worth trying.

Sometimes four tools are enough.

entry.title: Re: Intercompany Invoicing Errors - Report
entry.content: Is that currently Intercompany invoices not flowing in vfx3?  If it is not flowing, the first check you need to carry out is comparing both the billing document type configuration in VOFA

entry.title: Re: VF44 - Edit Revenue List - rounding
entry.content:
It could be due to various reasons like

Currency rounding
SAP rounds values at different stages (sales order, billing, revenue recognition). If the revenue recognition program recalculates based on percentages or allocation rules, a small rounding difference (like 0.01) can appear.

Exchange rate differences
If foreign currency is involved, exchange rate rounding can cause minor discrepancies.

Distribution of revenue
When revenue is split across multiple line items or periods, the system distributes amounts proportionally. The sum of distributed values may differ slightly from the total due to rounding.

Having said that 0.01 differences are normal and expected in revenue recognition due to rounding. These differences do not indicate a financial error and are accepted in audit practice.

entry.title: Re: Problem installing CR for Visual Studio SP40
entry.content:
This is a data retrieval error rather than a Crystal runtime installation failure. The error tells you the table in your SQL statement could not be loaded. The error was not generated by the Crystal runtime -- it was generated by your DBMS and simply reported back to you via the Crystal runtime.

Investigate what that error means in the context of your DBMS. If this is SQL Server, then one of the common causes of error 2229 is "Corrupted Installation Package" but that refers to the SQL Server installation package -- not the SAP installation package.

entry.title: Re: Optimizer is failing in case of Inbound Consignment O...
entry.content: The carrier is present in the consignment order but not properly tied to the optimizer’s usable resources. That is why removing TSPS_KEY makes it work. The fix is to check carrier assignment in resource master data and ensure consistency across FU, CO, lane, and resource.

entry.title: Re: Update settlement rule of maintenance order
entry.content: EXIT_SAPLCOI1_027 is too narrow. Use BAdI ORDER_SETTLEMENT_RULE (for settlement rule creation) and optionally WORKORDER_UPDATE (for consistency checks). That way, your logic applies regardless of whether the order is released via GUI, Web Dynpro, or Fiori.

entry.title: Agentic Joule for developer: It’s all about YOUR CHOICE
entry.content:
At Sapphire, SAP made one theme impossible to miss: the future is agentic, the ERP is autonomous, and the SAP Business AI platform is turning into the foundation for how this intelligence gets delivered across the portfolio. Joule for Developers is part of that bigger story.

While some technology announcements feel like feature updates, this one feels more like a launch moment — the point where the plot twist. Over the past year, ABAP AI has been proving its value in the practical trenches: explaining old code, accelerating test work, helping with findings, and making cloud development easier. Useful? Absolutely. But still mostly assistive. What Sapphire makes clear is that the next chapter is far more ambitious: ABAP AI is evolving from a set of smart skills into an agent-led development model — one built around choice: your choice of your preferred IDE between ABAP Development Tools on Eclipse or on VSCode, your partner of choice with your preferred AI assistant and orchestration, your choice of ABAP system from BTP till any S/4HANA version from 2021 up and your choice of consumption. And on top a much more serious strategy for RISE custom code migration.

That is the real shift here. Not just “more AI,” but AI that starts behaving like a coordinated system.

So this blog is not about one more feature. This one is about a full shift of thinking, with the upcoming general availability of the ABAP MCP server, open support for partner assistants and orchestrators, the general availability of ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code, a new consumption-based commercial model, and major progress in custom code migration, SAP Joule for Developers is turning into a developer platform built around one central idea: your choice.

Agentic Joule for Developers arrives with the upcoming general availability of the ABAP MCP server and an open partner strategy built around your choice of assistants and orchestrators

The most exciting signal in this story is the ABAP MCP server as of this quarter: Q2 2026. It opens ABAP development to a wider agentic universe by exposing ABAP capabilities in a way that assistants and orchestrators can truly work with. This is where the story becomes more than assistance and starts to feel like momentum. Instead of one closed experience, SAP is shaping a future in which customers and partners can bring their preferred AI assistant, their preferred orchestration layer, and their preferred model strategy into the flow. The result is a far more elegant promise for ABAP teams: SAP provides the ABAP intelligence, the MCP layer becomes the bridge, and you decide how the full agentic experience comes together.

ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code completes the promise of real IDE choice for ABAP developers

Choice becomes real the moment it reaches the daily developer experience, and that is exactly why ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code matters so much. It turns SAP’s commitment to IDE freedom into something tangible. Teams can stay in the environment that feels natural to them while still tapping into the full power of agentic ABAP. In this new model, Visual Studio Code becomes the creative workspace, the MCP layer becomes the action engine, and partner orchestration brings the journey together from first intent to finished Fiori application. It is a smoother, more modern, more natural way to build. For the first release the new Visual Studio Code is focused on ABAP Cloud but by the end of the year with the promise of the roadmap, the entire ABAP Classic support will be extended from Eclipse to VSCode IDE also.

Meeting you where you are with ABAP AI across private cloud, public cloud, on premise, and BTP

One of the most expected announcement for Joule for developer was not on the features but on the reach: the customers stretch across private cloud, public cloud, BTP and either on premise systems and starting June, Joule for Developers is being positioned to meet our customers where they are, across SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP BTP ABAP Environment, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition releases from 2021 onward. More than that for the RISE customers who fulfill the conditions, Joule for Developer will be available also on their On-Premise systems from 2021 onward. This changes the tone of adoption completely. Instead of waiting for every landscape decision to be perfect, teams can begin from the reality they already have and move forward from there.

Custom code migration steps into a new era with an agent-led toolchain for SAP S/4HANA transformation

If there is one place where agentic AI has to prove it can deliver more than clever assistance, it is custom code migration. And the CCM story now reads much more clearly as an end-to-end journey. It starts upstream with Cloud ALM, where project analysis helps teams understand the migration scope, structure the backlog, and identify where effort and risk are concentrated. That early project view matters because migration is never just a developer task. It is a transformation program. But once the scope is understood, the story shifts decisively into developer execution, and that is where the three CCM execution agents provided by Joule for developer come in.



1. Mass S/4 Custom Code Conversion Agent
This first agent planned for Q2 2026 is about scale. It helps developers take large volumes of custom code findings and move from assessment into executable remediation. Its role is to accelerate the mass conversion work that would otherwise remain trapped in analysis reports and manual follow-up.

2. Clean Core Adoption Agent
This second agent is about clean core transformation. It helps teams to move from technical correction to clean-core through recommendation and automatic fixes. This is planned for Q3 and Q4 2026.

3. Redesign and Modernization Agent (i.e. Dynpro-to-RAP)
This third and most complex agent is about application modernization and redesign. It focuses on the deeper transformation work, helping classic Dynpro-based patterns evolve toward a RAP-based future and turning modernization into a guided execution flow rather than a one-off rebuild effort. This agent is planned on the roadmap for late this year and early next year.

Together, these three agents create a much more structured CCM storyline: analyze the program in Cloud ALM, then execute the migration through conversion, clean-core adoption, and modernization. The timeline reinforces that progression. The first execution step begins in Q2 2026, broader execution capabilities expand through the second half of 2026, and the larger modernization promise continues to mature into 2027. What emerges is not just a better migration tool, but a much more credible migration motion from project analysis to developer delivery.

A new commercial model that lets you choose what you use, when you use it, and how far you scale it

Agentic Joule for developer is built as a choice-driven ecosystem also at the commercial layer. The model is designed for flexibility with consumption-based pricing, clear J4D activation, and differentiated paths for customers and partners. While the free promotion will end on 30th September the teams can work through ABAP Development Tools in Eclipse or VS Code, connect through the ABAP MCP Server and local ABAP MCP tools, at no extra cost, while taping into an agent pipeline powered by an open partner strategy at your choice that includes Microsoft, Amazon, Google, IBM, Anthropic and more. The commercial message is bold and unmistakable: Agentic Joule for developer is not just offering AI capabilities — it is giving customers and partners the freedom to build their own path, with their own tools, their own partners, and their own pace, paying only for the AI features which truly bring value to them. It feels far better suited to the rhythm of real AI adoption, where teams experiment, learn, expand, and refine over time rather than locking themselves into a single path on day one.

Bottom line: the future of ABAP looks less like a chatbot and more like an ecosystem

Put it all together and one message shines through powerfully: Agentic Joule for Developers is becoming a platform shaped around your choice. Your assistant. Your orchestrator. Your IDE. Your landscape. Your migration path. Your pace. And that is exactly what makes this new chapter feel so powerful: ABAP AI is growing up — from answering questions to orchestrating work, from isolated skills to partner-powered ecosystems, and from code assistance to full custom code migration strategy.

entry.title: Re: How to Implement SAP Business One projects successfully
entry.content:
Great breakdown on the implementation steps. Having run 20+ SMB go-lives in the Hyderabad market, I'd add one critical nuance: the go-live week is where SMBs lose the most money, and it's almost never a technical issue — it's a data readiness problem.

Three failure patterns I see repeatedly:

1. Master data validation is skipped under time pressure.

Teams migrate from Tally/legacy spreadsheets and the item master is "good enough" on cutover night. Three weeks later, inventory reports are useless because unit of measure conversions weren't standardized, batch costs don't reconcile, and your finance team is flying blind on actual landed costs per SKU.

The fix: run a parallel inventory count in the old system AND SAP B1 for 2 weeks before go-live. Any variance >2% stops go-live until resolved. More importantly — validate your batch cost data against physical stock. The gap between system cost and actual cost is where most pharma and manufacturing SMBs hemorrhage margin post-go-live, and they don't even see it until month-end close.

2. User training is treated as a checkbox — especially around real-time dashboards.

SMB owners assume the consultant will "handle it." What actually happens: 3 PowerPoint slides, a rushed demo, and a team that can log in but can't process a goods receipt — let alone interpret a live inventory KPI dashboard. The result: workarounds that corrupt data, followed by 6 months of cleanup where nobody trusts the numbers.

Better model: identify 2–3 power users per department in week 1. Train them deeply on both transactions AND real-time reporting. They need to understand what the system is telling them — stock-out risk, batch age, cycle count variance — not just how to enter data. If your team can't read the dashboard on Day 1, you've already lost the implementation ROI.

3. No cutover rollback plan — and no data integrity gate.

If something breaks on go-live night, what's the decision tree? Who decides to rollback vs. push through? This sounds obvious but almost no SMB implementation documents this. The result: 48 hours of chaos before the inevitable rollback — which costs more than if you'd planned for it.

Add a data integrity gate to your checklist: before go-live, run a batch cost gap analysis across all material groups. Compare system unit costs vs. last 3 purchase orders. If the gap exceeds 5%, you have a data quality problem that will cascade through every report, every KPI, and every management decision for the next quarter.

The Go-Live Checklist That Actually Works:

  • Parallel run confirmation — both systems running simultaneously for at least 5 business days
  • Master data validation report — zero unresolved UoM mismatches, batch cost reconciliation complete
  • Batch cost gap analysis — system cost vs. actual cost variance <5% across all material groups
  • Real-time dashboard validation — KPIs (inventory turnover, stock-out risk, FG batch age) returning accurate data
  • Rollback decision criteria — who decides, under what conditions, documented and signed off
  • Data freeze window — no master data changes 48 hours before cutover
  • Go-live support team — internal champion + consultant available for 72 hours straight
  • First-week close check — finance runs month-end in SAP B1 within 5 days

The single best investment: A formal 3-day parallel run where the team processes every real transaction in BOTH systems simultaneously. Painful? Yes. But teams that use this checklist average 3–4 days of post-go-live stabilization. Teams that skip it average 3–4 weeks.

For pharma and manufacturing companies — especially those with batch-managed inventory — the batch cost gap analysis is non-negotiable. If you don't know your real cost position at go-live, every downstream report is suspect. We've seen companies discover ₹15–40 lakh in cost variances that were invisible in Tally but immediately surfaced once SAP B1's real-time visibility kicked in.

What specific module are you struggling with? Happy to go deeper on any of these.

— Venkata Siva Reddy Polu, TEKROI
SAP B1 implementation for Hyderabad pharma and manufacturing SMBs | TekAI: AI-powered operations intelligence for SAP B1

entry.title: Re: EPPM Business Requirements , S4 Hana Private cloud
entry.content:
Hello!

SAP EPPM (Enterprise Portfolio and Project Management) is not natively designed to serve as a pre-sales / pre-contract lifecycle tool. However, a viable end-to-end solution is achievable through a combination of SAP EPPM's Commercial Project Management (CPM) module, SAP S/4HANA Customer Management (embedded CRM), and targeted configuration/enhancements.

What EPPM (CPM) Offers:

SAP Commercial Project Management (CPM), which is an integral component of the EPPM solution, plays a central role in the commercial lifecycle. Its scope explicitly starts from bid preparation and financial planning for the bid, and continues through to project handover. Within the CPM Master Project, a bid structure can be created and multiple financial plan versions can be modelled, compared, and an approved version marked as current before transfer to Project Systems for execution.

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-s-4hana-overview-on-enterprise-portfolio-and-project-management/ba-p/13479645

https://community.sap.com/t5/product-lifecycle-management-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-s-4hana-financial-planning-with-commercial-project-management/ba-p/13462771

Gaps and enhancements required

Tender status workflow: CPM has project status management, but a formal tendering workflow (e.g. Draft → Submitted → Evaluated → Awarded/Rejected) would need configuration of custom status profiles and, for approval workflows, SAP Business Workflow or SAP Build Process Automation.

Tender monitoring dashboards: Portfolio Management enhancements in recent releases focus on OData APIs and planning use cases in combination with SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), which can be leveraged to build tender pipeline dashboards. https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/enterprise-portfolio-and-project-management-in-sap-s-4hana-cloud-private/ba-p/14349843

Recommendation: Configure CPM Master Projects as tender objects. Use custom project type "Tender" with dedicated status schemas. Integrate with DMS for document approvals and SAC for monitoring.

What EPPM Offers, significant gap:

Lead generation and qualification is not a native EPPM capability. EPPM is fundamentally a project and portfolio execution tool, not a CRM front-end. There is no standard object within EPPM/CPM for creating, qualifying, or scoring inbound leads.

Recommended approach:

Within S/4HANA, SAP offers an integrated process that starts with lead generation, integrates opportunity management, and consistently maps the process through quotation and order management to invoicing, all within the S/4HANA landscape, avoiding the need for a separate CRM platform.

In SAP S/4HANA Customer Management, leads are managed through roles and registered in the system with the "Lead" role. If the opportunity is won, they are recorded as an actual customer. The solution supports creating, updating, and tracking opportunities; monitoring status (open, won, lost); saving opportunity-related documents and notes; and tracking activities such as meetings, calls, and emails. https://community.sap.com/t5/crm-and-cx-blog-posts-by-members/opportunity-management-in-s-4hana-customer-management-and-crm/ba-p/13997215

What EPPM Offers, via CPM Object Links:

CPM Master Projects can hold multiple S/4HANA business objects including Sales Order Headers, Sales Order Inquiries, and Opportunities, acting as an umbrella container linking commercial objects to project execution. This means once an opportunity exists in S/4HANA Customer Management or CRM, it can be linked into a CPM Master Project, providing the handoff from pre-sales to project delivery. https://community.sap.com/t5/product-lifecycle-management-blog-posts-by-members/improved-eppm-processes-with-s-4hana-2020-fps01-integration-between/ba-p/13518560

Gaps:

The conversion of a Lead → Opportunity → CPM Tender Project is not a single automated standard flow. It requires configuration of object links and, ideally, a custom BTP workflow or Fiori extension to automate the transition.

When a CPM Master Project is created from a Portfolio Item, the option to manually add other business objects (such as Opportunities) directly in CPM is disabled, which is a known limitation when using the PPM-CPM integration path. This must be factored into the solution design.

Key recommendations:

Do not attempt to use EPPM alone to cover lead and opportunity management, this creates unsupported customisation debt. Use S/4HANA Customer Management (or SAP Sales Cloud for larger CRM needs) as the front-end for leads and opportunities.

Use CPM as the bridge: the CPM Master Project is the right object to represent a tender/bid, and it natively links to opportunities from the CRM layer.

Invest in a BTP workflow to automate the Lead → Opportunity → CPM Tender Project transition with status-based triggers, rather than relying on manual object linking.

Build the pre-contract pipeline view in SAP Analytics Cloud, leveraging the PPM Portfolio layer as the grouping mechanism.

entry.title: Re: S/4HANA Public Cloud Inbound ASN：Global S/4HANA OP 的...
entry.content:
Good day @Joey3 

Thank you for your question.

Based on the translation provided, see below.

I would recommend you don’t pass DELVRY03 directly into the receiving ASN interface if the target standard expects DESADV/DELVR07. The global system can continue to send DELVRY03 but CPI should convert it into the supported DELVRY07 structure before sending it to the receiving system.

See KBA 3478211 - Central KBA for DESADV idoc - SAP for Me  and 3705980 - Incoterm location 1 (INCO2) not inherited in inbound delivery created with DELVRY07 - SAP for Me for more insights.

 

For your current scope, where there are only standard fields and no HU, serial number, or custom extension, the mapping complexity should normally be low to medium. The main work you have is to map the control record, header, partner, reference, item, quantity, UoM, and date fields correctly. If handling units, serial numbers, batch split, packaging hierarchy, or subcontracting components are added later, the complexity will increase. KBA 2753425 provides details on field specific behaviour in DELVRY07/DESADV processing.

As a next step, I recommend the following:

  1. Keep DELVRY03 only as the source format from the global system.
  2. Build CPI mapping from DELVRY03 to DELVRY07.
  3. Validate the payload you mapped before sending it to the receiving system.
  4. Test inbound delivery creation with simple ASN data first.
  5. Add negative tests for missing PO, wrong PO item, invalid material, wrong quantity and duplicate ASN.
  6. Monitor the message in CPI and then verify the created inbound delivery in the receiving system.
  7. Use the above mentioned KBA’s for DESADV processing issues and for if you observe item sequence after inbound creation.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/588780cab2774a7ab9fffca3a7f919fe/9e99ba26b4d24b04b9704bd1c2781f5f.html?locale=en-US 

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/aff0b3f5f46c42a2b2c0fabfc233bab2/f176b6535fe6b74ce10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US 

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-sourcing-and-procurement-es/configuring-direct-procurement-with-inbound-delivery_c6b73bbe-65ae-4782-a020-9306f582cf66 

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Cash Flow Report Direct Method in S4 Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hello @sanne_larsen

Thanks for the follow-up question.

By cash relevant account logic, I mean identifying the actual cash movement from bank, cash and bank clearing accounts, then classifying the offsetting business transaction into the correct direct cash flow category. This is different from ECC style reason code logic.

In Public Cloud, reason codes are mainly used for payment differences. They are not recommended design objects for classifying a direct method cash flow report.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/1bbe9ae6af1a431aa8584f6edd919918.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/a8d0f49995394451882809d7a0171f6a.html?locale=en-US

For an internal direct cash flow report, the better approach for you is to use Liquidity Item Derivation. Define liquidity items such as customer receipts, supplier payments, payroll payments, etc. Then configure derivative rules and query sequences so the system assigns the right liquidity item to cash relevant postings. KBA 3670979 provides details on how to configure liquidity item derivation rules.

 

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Returnable Packaging Materials in SAP EWM - Closed Loop
entry.content:
Introduction

Reusable materials, often called Returnable Transport Packaging (RTP), are an important part of modern supply chains. In industries such as consumer packaged goods (CPG), automotive, retail, life sciences, and industrial manufacturing, companies use assets like pallets, crates, totes, racks, drums, and containers to move goods efficiently and safely.

In CPG, especially food and beverage, examples include CHEP pallets, Euro pallets, and totes. These materials help products move smoothly across suppliers, plants, warehouses, and retailers. Similar models are used in other industries to improve handling, reduce waste, and support more consistent operations.

Many companies prefer reusable packaging over single-use alternatives because it can reduce cost, improve asset utilization, and support sustainability goals. In some cases, these assets are leased or pooled through providers such as CHEP, RPC, and Euro Pool System, giving companies access to shared packaging networks without having to own all the assets themselves.

CHEP pallet

The business model often depends on who owns the packaging:

Ownership model What it means
Customer-ownedThe customer owns the packaging materials and provides them to suppliers as needed.
Service provider-ownedA logistics or pooling provider owns the materials and makes them available for a rental or usage fee.
Supplier-ownedThe supplier owns the packaging used to deliver products to the customer.

SAP Returnable Packaging Management

SAP Returnable Packaging Management(SAP RPM) is a cloud-based solution that helps companies track, plan, and manage returnable packaging materials across closed-loop supply chain operations.

Closed-loop packaging operations enable companies to move goods in reusable containers that are returned, tracked, and redeployed across the supply chain, improving visibility, reducing waste, and supporting more efficient packaging management.

Without SAP RPM

Even without SAP Returnable Packaging Management, organizations can design a practical closed-loop packaging process in SAP S/4HANA and EWM by defining returnable packaging materials, managing them as special stock( V - Customer Returnable Packaging Stock). 

This setup demonstrates how returnable packaging moves in a closed loop between shipper and customer.

Goods Receipt, Goods Issue, and Returns Process for Returnable Packaging

 

Goods Receipt Process

In this example, materials are externally procured from suppliers or stocks received from other plants, and delivered in reusable packaging materials such as pallets, bins, or containers. The objective is to show how the reusable packaging can be received, tracked, and managed in SAP S/4HANA and EWM as part of a closed-loop process.

Prerequisites

In the Embedded/decentralized EWM system, activate the required Business Configuration Sets using transaction SCPR20. In particular, activate BC Set /SCWM/DLV_INBOUND_PACK to create the necessary item types and related settings required for packaging material line creation in inbound deliveries.

Master Data

a. Define packaging materials in SAP S/4HANA

Create the reusable packaging materials in SAP S/4HANA. Depending on the business requirement, these materials may represent serialized or non-serialized containers.

Material Type - VERP

 

b. Define EWM Goods Movement Bin

Create a virtual storage bin to receive and issue these packaging materials. In this example, the bin PACK_MATERIAL is set up and assigned for goods movement bin determination using transaction /SCWM/GMBIN_DET.

 

Configuration

EWM

a. Activate Automatic Packaging Item Generation for Inbound Delivery Item Types

SPRO path: SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Goods Receipt Process → Automatically Generate Packaging for Inbound Deliveries

 

b. Copy the standard status profile /SCWM/INB_PRD_PAC to a custom status profile. This is required to deactivate status type DPT (Putaway), which avoids an additional putaway step for the packaging material and ensures goods receipt bin determination is driven via /SCWM/GMBIN_DET.

SPRO path: SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Cross-Process Settings → Delivery-Warehouse Request → Define Status Profiles

c. Assign the custom status profile to the IPAC item type.

SPRO path: SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Goods Receipt Process → Inbound Delivery → Define Item Types for Inbound Delivery Process

 

d. Maintain the packaging material type and the assigned number range type.

SPRO path: SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Cross-Process Settings → Handling Units → Define Packaging Material Types for Warehousing

Goods Issue Process

In this example, I considered a scenario where reusable packaging materials are shipped to the customer.

Prerequisites

In the decentralized EWM system, activate the required BC Sets using transaction SCPR20, and activate BC set /SCWM/DLV_OUTBOUND_PACK to create the necessary item types and related configuration to support packaging material line creation in outbound deliveries.

Master Data

EWM

a. Define the EWM Goods Movement Bin

In /SCWM/GMBIN_DET, assign storage bin PACK_MATERIAL to item type OPHU.

Configuration

a. Activate packaging items generation for Item type

SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Goods Issue Process -> Outbound Delivery -> Automatically Generate Packaging Items

b. Activate Automatic Packing in Delivery Type - S4

SPRO path: Logistics Execution → Shipping → Deliveries → Define Delivery Types

c. Maintain Item Category Determination - S4

SPRO path: Logistics Execution → Shipping → Deliveries → Define Item Categories for Deliveries

 

d. Maintain Item Category Copy Routines for Delivery Type–Sales Order Type Combinations - S4

SPRO path: Logistics Execution → Shipping → Copying Control → Specify Copy Control for Deliveries


 

Define Schedule Line Category 

Assign Schedule Line Category to Item Category

System Demo

 

Goods Receipt Process

I created a purchase order and delivery in S/4, then distributed the inbound delivery to EWM. During goods receipt in EWM, a packaging material line item is generated automatically on the inbound delivery.

Purchase order Creation

PO# 4500017291

Inbound Delivery # 180001104

EWM Inbound Delivery

Pack and post goods receipt. New packaging line item should get added in the inbound delivery.

I also posted Inventory for Packaging Material PKE-090

Verify the stock in warehouse monitor, inbound delivery should be blank in the stock

Verify S4 delivery for packaging material line item addition

Goods Issue Process

I created a sales order and delivery in S/4, then distributed the outbound delivery to EWM. During goods issue in EWM, a packaging material line item is generated automatically on the outbound delivery.

Sales Order # 15553

Outbound Delivery # 80120258

EWM Outbound Delivery # 310121

Pick and post goods issue for this delivery to add the packaging material line item. I packed 2 HUs using 2 different packaging materials.

Stock in warehouse monitor

 
 
Once the material is issued to the customer, the system creates an entry like the one shown below. It then posts movement type 621, transferring the material from unrestricted stock to special stock.

Returnable Packaging Pickup

To illustrate the returns process, I created a returns sales order with order type AT for packaging material PKE-090, which is returned by the customer and received back into warehouse stock.

 

Create Returns Delivery

 Movement type in this case would be 622 

Return Delivery # 84000046 

Post Goods Receipt in EWM

 

Refresh Stock Overview

Special Stock will move to unrestricted stock

This approach helps you track packaging inventory at the customer site and request its return when required. If the customer decides to retain the packaging material, it can be billed using sales order type RPI.

Use the MB58 report for reporting and inventory tracking.

For rental items, this information can also be shared with leasing companies such as CHEP and IFCO through file transfers, IDocs, or other integration mechanisms.

Thanks for reading this blog post.

References:

Generation of Delivery Items for Packaging Materials | SAP Help Portal

Learn SAP skills | SAP Learning

entry.title: What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2605
entry.content:
With this release of SAP Asset Performance Management, we’re continuing to deliver on the feedback and improvement requests we hear from our customers and partners every day.

Several of the innovations highlighted in this update were directly influenced by customer feedback and SAP Continuous Influence requests — including the new Industrial System Category configuration capabilities, one of the most highly requested enhancements in this area, the hotly anticipated AI-powered anomaly detection, and field extensibility capabilities for the Explore Technical Objects app, so customers can bring customer-specific ERP fields directly into SAP Asset Performance Management.

If you’d like a broader look at what’s planned for the second half of the year, join me and the APM product management team as we present our upcoming SAP APM roadmap webcast on June 3rd at 4pm CET, where the we'll share upcoming innovations and answer your live questions. Register here.

 

Asset Data and Information Management

More Flexible Industrial System Modeling with Industrial System Categories

What if your Industrial System structure could follow a standard where needed, but still adapt to the way your business actually models assets?

With the new Industrial System Category configuration, SAP Asset Performance Management gives customers more control over how Industrial Systems and Industrial System Templates are structured, from aspect relationships to technical object assignments, including ISO-aligned and customer-specific modeling rules.

Challenge

Industrial System modeling needs to support different customer requirements. Some customers want to follow a standard-aligned structure, while others need more flexibility to reflect their own asset structures and modeling rules.

Previously, fixed modeling behavior made it harder to adapt Industrial Systems to customer-specific requirements, especially when defining relationships between aspects such as function, product, and location, or when assigning technical objects such as equipment and functional locations.

Solution

With Industrial System Categories, users can define how Industrial Systems and Industrial System Templates should be structured.

Categories make it possible to configure:

  • Relationships between aspect types
  • Which technical objects can be assigned
  • Whether single or multiple assignments are allowed
  • Reference Designator System formatting
  • Whether standard-compliant behavior should be applied

SAP provides two pre-shipped categories:

Default ISO category
For structures that follow the ISO 81346-compliant standard. This category applies stricter modeling rules, such as allowing assignments only to the Product Aspect and only one instance of equipment or functional location.

Non-default ISO category
For existing or more flexible structures that follow parts of the ISO-related modeling approach, but not the stricter default setup. Existing Industrial Systems and Industrial System Templates are automatically assigned to this category during upgrade, so they continue to work as expected.

Configuration Manager view showing customizable system category definitions, including ISO-compliant and non-standard categories, with options to activate and set defaults.

Customers can also create their own categories. For example, a customer can allow equipment to be assigned to a Function Aspect, functional locations to a Location Aspect, and define whether multiple assignments are allowed.

Once a category is active, it can be used when creating Industrial Systems or Industrial System Templates. If marked as default, it is proposed automatically, while users can still choose another active category if needed.

Outcome

Industrial System Categories help customers model systems in a way that better fits their business, while still supporting governance and standard alignment.

This gives customers:

  • More flexible Industrial System modeling
  • Support for ISO-aligned and customer-specific structures
  • Better control over aspect relationships and technical object assignments
  • Smooth upgrade behavior for existing Industrial Systems
  • Consistent modeling rules across Industrial Systems and Templates

In short, customers no longer have to choose between standardization and flexibility. With Industrial System Categories, they can support both. See it in action in this video:

Configuring the Industrial System Category

and see the SAP Help Portal Industrial System Category.

Field extensibility for the “Explore Technical Objects” app

For many customers, critical asset information is stored in custom ERP fields tailored to their business processes. But without extensibility support in APM, that information remains disconnected from reliability analysis and day-to-day decision-making.

With the new field extensibility capabilities for the Explore Technical Objects app, customers can now bring customer-specific ERP fields directly into SAP Asset Performance Management and make them available across Technical Object details, filters, and list views.

Challenge

The Explore Technical Objects application previously did not support extensibility for customer-specific fields.

As a result:

  • Custom fields from SAP S/4HANA or SAP ERP could not easily be replicated into APM
  • Reliability Engineers could not view customer-specific business attributes within the application
  • Important operational context remained separated from APM processes and analysis

This made it harder for customers to tailor APM applications to their own business requirements.

Solution

The new extensibility capabilities introduce extension points that allow custom fields from SAP S/4HANA or SAP ERP to be integrated into APM through the Integration Layer.

The solution includes:

  • Extensibility support at CDS view level
  • SAP CAP framework extensibility capabilities
  • Key User Extensibility support for Technical Object applications
  • Support for customer-specific fields in detail pages, filters, and list views

This allows customers to seamlessly integrate additional ERP business attributes into the APM data model and expose them directly within the Explore Technical Objects app.

Outcome

With field extensibility, customers can now enrich Technical Object information with business-specific ERP data and make that information available directly within APM applications.

This provides:

  • Better visibility into customer-specific business context
  • More flexible Technical Object analysis
  • Extended filtering and list reporting capabilities
  • Reusable custom fields across multiple APM applications
  • Improved business decision-making by combining standard APM data with customer-specific ERP information

For Reliability Engineers and business users, this means working with a more complete picture of the asset without needing to switch between systems.

Watch this video:

Watch the video: Extending the “Explore Technical Objects” app

For more information, see the SAP Help Portal Replicating Custom Fields of Explore Technical Objects from SAP Backend Systems.

 

Asset Health Monitoring

Anomaly Detection (Early Adopter Access)

This month's release brings one of the most anticipated additions to SAP Asset Performance Management: Anomaly Detection. This new capability, currently available through Early Adopter Access, marks a significant step forward for condition-based maintenance — and it doesn't require AI expertise to use. Let's take a closer look.

Note: Details on how to get Early Adopter access are described on the SAP Asset Performance Management Help Portal .

AI-Powered Anomaly Detection for Condition-Based Maintenance

Challenge

Asset health is rarely a single-sensor problem.

In practice, monitoring an asset's condition means working with many indicators — temperature, vibration, pressure, flow rates — all of which vary depending on the operational context. When that context changes frequently, defining static threshold rules becomes difficult, if not impossible.

To make things harder:

  • A single indicator is rarely enough to reliably signal a developing fault
  • The operational context of an asset can shift frequently, making fixed alert thresholds unreliable
  • Known failure events are rare, which makes it hard to distinguish normal from abnormal behavior across a fleet

Traditional rule-based monitoring approaches can handle simple cases, but when asset behavior is multivariate, dynamic, and context-dependent, rule definitions quickly become unmanageable.

Solution

With the new Anomaly Detection capability in SAP Asset Performance Management, business users — without any AI expertise — can train anomaly detection models in a few guided steps.

Indicator Monitoring view showing multiple asset indicators and anomaly score trends over time, enabling reliability engineers to correlate sensor behavior and anomalies in a single chart

The Manage Anomaly Detection Models application uses the unsupervised Isolation Forest algorithm to identify unusual patterns across multivariate sensor data streams. Here's what makes it practical:

  • Notification-guided training: Existing maintenance notification data can be used to identify known failure periods and select the most relevant training data — so the model learns what "abnormal" looks like with precision.
  • Flexible training configuration: Define up to 20 training periods per model, with time-based and indicator value-based exclusions to filter out irrelevant data (maintenance windows, operational context changes) and sharpen detection accuracy.
  • Fleet-level models: Train a single model across multiple assets that share similar attributes and operational context, enabling consistent monitoring across an entire asset fleet.
  • Simulation before deployment: Once trained, simulate the model's performance on historical data. Adjust the anomaly score threshold interactively to find the right balance between sensitivity and false positives — before releasing the model into production.
  • Seamless integration with monitoring rules: Released models feed directly into anomaly detection scoring rules and monitoring rules, generating anomaly scores that trigger alerts and notifications when defined thresholds are exceeded.

 Outcome

Anomaly Detection transforms how you approach condition-based maintenance in SAP APM:

  • Implement condition-based maintenance strategies where rule-based monitoring would fail or be impractical to configure
  • Detect early signs of asset degradation across many indicators simultaneously
  • Identify bad actors in an asset fleet and act before failures occur
  • Reduce manual IoT data analysis — the model handles the pattern recognition
  • Optimize maintenance schedules by performing work only when the asset's condition warrants it

For more information, refer to the SAP Help Portal — Anomaly Detection. Watch this space for a dedicated enablement blog and webcast coming soon. 

 

Bringing Maintenance Context into Indicator Monitoring

What good is an indicator chart if it only tells part of the story?

Challenge

Previously, Reliability Engineers monitoring asset health through indicators and condition data often had to switch between multiple applications to understand what actually happened around a spike, drop, or anomaly.

Maintenance notifications and work orders existed — but not in the same context as the indicator trend itself.

As a result:

  • Notifications and work orders were not visible directly within the indicator chart
  • Users had to navigate across multiple applications to investigate issues
  • Correlating maintenance activity with indicator behavior was more time-consuming
  • Important operational context could easily be missed

This created fragmented visibility between condition monitoring and maintenance execution, making investigations slower and less efficient.

With the latest enhancement, maintenance notifications and work orders can now be viewed directly alongside indicator trends, helping Reliability Engineers connect asset behavior with maintenance activity more quickly and efficiently.

Solution

The enhanced indicator monitoring experience now integrates maintenance context directly into the indicator chart.

Maintenance notifications and orders can now be displayed directly in the indicator chart, helping users correlate asset condition trends with maintenance activities in one view.

Notifications and work orders can be visualized alongside indicator trends, helping users understand:

  • When maintenance activities occurred
  • Whether actions were triggered by condition changes
  • How maintenance execution relates to asset behavior over time

By combining operational and maintenance context in a single view, users can investigate asset events more efficiently and with fewer navigation steps.

Outcome

With maintenance information embedded directly into indicator monitoring, Reliability Engineers gain a more connected view of asset performance and maintenance history.

This helps customers:

  • Improve correlation between asset health and maintenance actions
  • Accelerate root cause investigation
  • Reduce context switching between applications
  • Make faster and more informed maintenance decisions
  • Gain better visibility into the effectiveness of maintenance activities

Instead of analyzing trends and maintenance activities separately, users can now view them together — in context.

 

Stay Informed and Get Involved

  • Find out what is coming up in the second half of the year in SAP Asset Performance Management - Join our roadmap webcast on June 3rd at 4pm CET by registering here.
  • We've recently revamped our ​SAP Community for APM page. Check out this one stop shop for an overview of upcoming enablement sessions, content and learning opportunities for APM. ​Subscribe get notified of updates.
  • Explore our latest APM blog posts here.
  • Find out what's up and coming in the SAP Road Map Explorer.
  • For the full feature documentation for 2605, visit the SAP Help Portal .
  • Send us your improvement requests. Submit or vote for the improvement you would like to see in the SAP Continuous Influence session.
  • Check the latest update of the APM link collection.

See you for the next update!

entry.title: Re: how to store/reuse all bids & enable backup suppliers
entry.content:
Good day @Goodkidmart-11 

Thank you for your question.

The clean design is to separate bid audit history from operational purchasing reuse.

All winning and losing bid history should remain in Ariba Sourcing as the sourcing audit trail. The operational purchasing system should only reuse prices that are deliberately approved as valid sources, for example through purchasing info records, source lists, or contracts. Note that in the 4BL sourcing process, awarded quotations are sent back to the cloud, which means the standard process award driven, not designed to automatically convert every non awarded bid into an active operational source.

For valid winning prices, use purchasing info records or outline agreements. A purchasing info record stores supplier and material specific purchasing data such as price, conditions and planned delivery time, and is used in the PO process.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/c63a1fca5f6d46d7b0e0de97680bce3d.html?locale=en-US

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-sourcing-and-procurement-zh/maintaining-purchasing-info-record_a3b03f6b-d796-43f2-88e5-cdaa282ac99d

For fast supplier switching, maintain backup suppliers as valid alternative source, not only as old non awarded bids. Use Manage Purchasing Info Records to maintain backup supplier prices and  

Manage Source Lists to control valid sources by material and plant.    

I recommend the following steps:

  1. Keep all bid history in Ariba Sourcing for audit.
  2. Transfer and reused awarded supplier prices through the standard quotation to PO or quotation to PIR process.
  3. For backup suppliers, create approved purchasing info records or contracts with validity dates.
  4. Maintain source lists for your main and backup suppliers.
  5. Test supplier switching in the PO and confirm the correct price is proposed.
  6. Check KBA 2887939 if the info record is not created or updated from the quotation.
  7. Should the PO not adopt the awarded Ariba quotation price and instead keeps the PR price, check KBA 3733443 for insights.

 

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: SAP Snowflake: How to Store and Manage Secrets for APIs, Database Credentials, and Keys
entry.content:
Previous Blog on SAP Databricks on How to Store secrets : https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/how-to-store-and-manage-sap-secrets-using-sap-databricks-cli-a-developer-s/ba-p/14323394
 
SAP Snowflake Secrets is a feature in Snowflake that lets you securely store and manage sensitive information—like passwords, API keys, tokens, or credentials—directly inside Snowflake, instead of hardcoding them in scripts or applications.

As shown in below, if you need to bring your own data into SAP snowflake without Zero-copy concept. These secrets needs to be applied to pull the data from various SAP Applications. ￼

In SAP Snowflake, secrets are schema-level objects used to store sensitive information like API keys or passwords. If you need to create, update or delete a secret, follow the steps below.

OAuth2 Secret

CREATE OR REPLACE SECURITY INTEGRATION my_oauth2_integration
  TYPE = API_AUTHENTICATION
  AUTH_TYPE = OAUTH2
  OAUTH_CLIENT_ID = '1234567890'
  OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET = '1234567890'
  OAUTH_TOKEN_ENDPOINT = 'https://oauth.com/token'
  OAUTH_AUTHORIZATION_ENDPOINT = 'https://oauth.com/authorize'
  OAUTH_ALLOWED_SCOPES = ('scope1', 'scope2')
  ENABLED = TRUE
  COMMENT = 'OAuth2 integration for external API';

Steps:

  1. Ensure a security integration for OAuth2 exists.
  2. Create the secret:
  3. Use this for APIs requiring OAuth client credentials or authorization code grant flows.
CREATE OR REPLACE SECRET my_oauth2_secret
TYPE = OAUTH2
API_AUTHENTICATION = my_oauth2_integration
OAUTH_SCOPES = ('scope1', 'scope2')
COMMENT = 'OAuth2 credentials for external API';

Basic Authentication Secret

Steps:

  • Store username and password securely:

CREATE OR REPLACE SECRET service_now_creds_pw
 TYPE = PASSWORD
 USERNAME = 'jsmith1'
 PASSWORD = 'W3dr@fg*7B1c4j'
 COMMENT = 'ServiceNow basic auth credentials';

Generic String Secret

Steps:

  • Store any sensitive string (e.g., API key):
CREATE OR REPLACE SECRET my_string_secret
 TYPE = GENERIC_STRING
 SECRET_STRING = 'my-secret-api-key-123'
 COMMENT = 'API key for service authentication';

Access Control (Privileges control usage:)
GRANT READ ON SECRET my_api_secret TO ROLE my_role;
Only roles with permission can use the secret.

Steps to Delete a Secret

  • Use the DROP SECRET Command Execute the following SQL command to delete a specific secret:

DROP SECRET <secret_name>;

Use SHOW SECRETS  to list all secrets and confirm the target secret exists:

SHOW SECRETS
